Name: Source Note
Mahi Shukla: The passages are adapted from Nathaniel Hawthorne, The Blithedale Romance. Originally published in 1852.
Five-and-twenty years ago, at the epoch of this story, there dwelt, a man whom we shall call Fauntleroy.
A man of wealth, and magnificent tastes, and prodigal expenditure. His home might almost be styled a palace, his habits in the ordinary sense, princely.
column3: of prodigal expenditure. home might a in the sense, princely.

Mahi Shukla: His whole being seemed to have crystallized itself into an external splendor, wherewith he glittered in the eyes of the world.

He saw himself in imminent peril of losing all that had heretofore distinguished him; and, conscious of no innate worth to fall back upon, he recoiled from this calamity, with the instinct of a soul shrinking from annihilation.
column3: himself imminent peril of losing that had and, of innate to back upon, he recoiled this with a shrinking

Mahi Shukla: Fauntleroy's guilt was discovered. He fled; his wife perished by the necessity of her innate nobleness, in its alliance with a being so ignoble; and betwixt her mother's death and her father's ignominy, his daughter was left worse than orphaned.
column3: was discovered. wife by the nobleness, its a being ignoble; and her than orphaned.
